threats.title: Public _addCredIdPerAddress and _removeCredIdPerAddress Allow Anyone to Corrupt Cred Tracking and Lock User Shares
threats.hypothesis_description: _addCredIdPerAddress and _removeCredIdPerAddress are declared public despite underscore-prefixed naming suggesting internal visibility. Any external account can call _removeCredIdPerAddress(credId, victim) to remove tracking entries, causing subsequent sell operations by the victim to revert in _updateCuratorShareBalance when it attempts to call _removeCredIdPerAddress again with stale index data (WrongCredId revert). The victim's shares and underlying ETH become permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code defect with zero prerequisites — any address can call these functions to corrupt any other user's tracking state, permanently preventing sells.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function _addCredIdPerAddress(uint256 credId_, address sender_) public and function _removeCredIdPerAddress(uint256 credId_, address sender_) public — both marked public with no access control

preconditions: Target victim holds at least one share in a credId
No special roles or balances required for attacker

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Functions named with underscore prefix (internal convention) but declared public with no modifiers
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — any time after a user holds shares

operational_questions: Can external _removeCredIdPerAddress desynchronize indexes enough to permanently block sells?
Can external _addCredIdPerAddress add duplicate entries causing further corruption?
Is there any recovery path for affected users?
validation_requirements: Call _removeCredIdPerAddress(credId, victim) externally, then have victim attempt sellShareCred
Verify _updateCuratorShareBalance reverts with WrongCredId or IndexOutofBounds
Confirm shares remain in shareBalance but are unsellable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify target with shares in credId X via getCuratorAddresses
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call _removeCredIdPerAddress(X, victim) to remove tracking entry
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim's sell attempts revert because _updateCuratorShareBalance calls _removeCredIdPerAddress with stale index
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim's ETH locked in bonding curve permanently
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — single transaction, no capital needed

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Any user's cred shares can be permanently locked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix; locked funds unrecoverable without code change

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic — call a public function with two parameters

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unable to sell their shares
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Systematic targeting of large share holders

threats.title: Unsigned Quantity in signatureClaim and merkleClaim Allows Minting Entire Supply with Single Valid Proof
threats.hypothesis_description: In PhiFactory.signatureClaim, the signature covers encodeData_ (expiresIn, minter, ref, verifier, artId, chainId, data) but does NOT cover mintArgs_ which contains quantity and imageURI. Similarly in merkleClaim, the merkle leaf only covers (minter, leafPart), not quantity. An eligible minter with a valid signature or merkle proof for quantity=1 can modify the quantity parameter to mint up to maxSupply - numberMinted tokens in a single transaction.
threats.investigation_rationale: The mint quantity is user-controlled and not covered by any cryptographic verification, allowing authorized minters to exceed intended mint amounts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: signatureClaim verifies signature over encodeData_ but uses mintArgs_.quantity from unverified calldata; merkleClaim verifies proof over (minter_, leafPart_) but quantity from mintArgs_ is unverified

preconditions: Attacker has a valid signature or merkle proof for at least 1 mint
Art has remaining supply (maxSupply - numberMinted > 1)
Attacker has enough ETH for the increased mintFee (quantity * per-unit fee)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: MintArgs struct (tokenId, quantity, imageURI) passed separately from signed/proven data
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — exploitable by any eligible minter
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time art has remaining supply

operational_questions: Can a user with a valid 1-quantity signature mint the entire remaining supply?
Are mint fees correctly calculated for the inflated quantity?
What is the economic impact of minting maxSupply in one transaction?
validation_requirements: Obtain valid signature for quantity=1, submit claim with quantity=maxSupply
Verify _validateAndUpdateClaimState only checks maxSupply not per-signature quantity
Confirm entire remaining supply can be minted

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain valid signature or merkle proof (legitimate eligibility)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Modify quantity in mintArgs to maxSupply - numberMinted
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Submit claim with inflated quantity and sufficient ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Receive entire remaining supply of NFTs
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None beyond being an eligible minter

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Art supply exhausted by single minter, other eligible users locked out
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All future claims for affected artId

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Low — modify one parameter in claim calldata
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Before art endTime and before supply exhausted

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Claims with quantity much larger than normal

threats.title: buyShareCredFor Enables Indefinite Sell-Lock Griefing Against Any Curator
threats.hypothesis_description: buyShareCredFor(credId, 1, victim, 0) buys 1 share on behalf of the victim, setting lastTradeTimestamp[credId][victim] = block.timestamp. This resets the victim's 10-minute SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD, preventing them from selling. An attacker can repeatedly call this (cost: 1 share at bonding curve price, which is cheapest at low supply) to indefinitely prevent any curator from selling their shares. batchBuyShareCred extends this to multiple creds simultaneously.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lock period is designed to prevent flash trading but is exploitable because a third party can trigger it for any address at minimal cost.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buyShareCredFor allows any msg.sender to buy for curator_; _handleTrade sets lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp on buy

preconditions: Target victim holds sellable shares in a credId
Attacker has enough ETH to buy 1 share (minimal cost at low supply)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Lock timestamp set for curator_ (not msg.sender) when buying on behalf of another
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Continuous — attacker must repeat every 10 minutes to maintain lock

operational_questions: What is the minimum cost to maintain the lock for 1 hour? 1 day?
Can the attacker recover cost by selling the shares they bought for the victim?
Can batchBuyShareCred be used to lock multiple creds simultaneously?
validation_requirements: Call buyShareCredFor(credId, 1, victim, 0), then have victim attempt sell within 10 minutes
Verify ShareLockPeriodNotPassed revert
Calculate bonding curve cost for repeated 1-share buys

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify target curator with valuable position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim unable to sell during entire griefing period
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If timed during price decline, victim suffers losses

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Stops when attacker stops funding; no permanent damage but temporal losses

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated small buyShareCredFor calls for same curator
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Curator unable to sell despite holding shares

threats.title: Shared credMerkleRoot Overwritten When New Art Created for Same CredId Invalidates Existing Merkle Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] is a single shared merkle root for all arts linked to a given cred. When _initializePhiArt is called for a new art with the same credId, it overwrites credMerkleRoot with the new merkle root hash. This invalidates all existing merkle proofs for previously created arts sharing that credId, preventing legitimate minters from claiming those arts.
threats.investigation_rationale: The system is designed to support multiple arts per credId (evidenced by _useExistingNFTContract path), but the merkle root storage is not per-art — it's per-cred, creating a destructive overwrite.

proof_of_signal.pattern: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash in _initializePhiArt overwrites any previous root; merkleClaim reads credMerkleRoot[art.credChainId][art.credId]

preconditions: Art A exists with MERKLE verification for credId X with root R1
Art B is created for same credId X with different root R2
Legitimate minters have proofs valid against R1 but not R2

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Single merkle root slot shared across all arts per credId
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires phiSigner to sign a new art creation (legitimate flow)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a new art is created for an existing credId

operational_questions: How often are multiple arts created for the same credId?
Can a malicious art creator intentionally trigger this to deny claims?
Is there a recovery mechanism for affected minters?
validation_requirements: Create Art A for credId=1 with merkle root R1
Create Art B for credId=1 with merkle root R2
Verify merkleClaim for Art A with proofs against R1 fails

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify existing MERKLE-verified art with unclaimed mints
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Obtain signed art creation data for the same credId with a different merkle root
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All pending merkle claims for previous arts are now invalid
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires legitimate art creation pathway (phiSigner signature)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Eligible minters permanently locked out of claiming
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires creating yet another art with the original root to restore
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: merkleClaim for all prior arts sharing that credId

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: credMerkleRoot changes for credIds with existing unclaimed arts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed merkle claims after new art creation
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Art creation targeting credIds with active claim periods

threats.title: Excess ETH Sent to PhiFactory.claim() Permanently Trapped — No Refund to Caller
threats.hypothesis_description: PhiFactory.claim() computes the exact mintFee and forwards only that amount via this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee} or this.signatureClaim{value: mintFee}. Any excess ETH (msg.value - mintFee) remains in the PhiFactory contract. Inside _processClaim, the refund logic checks (etherValue_ - mintFee) and sends to _msgSender(), but _msgSender() is address(this) when called via the self-call path, so refunds go back to the factory. The excess is only recoverable by the owner via withdraw().
threats.investigation_rationale: Users naturally send rounded ETH amounts or slightly over-estimate fees. The contract silently absorbs excess without refund, creating a persistent fund loss vector.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claim() calls this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee} — only mintFee forwarded; excess stays in factory. _processClaim refund to _msgSender() which is address(this) during self-call

preconditions: User calls claim() or batchClaim() with msg.value > exact mintFee
This is the normal user-facing claim path

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Self-call pattern changes msg.sender context, breaking refund logic
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner can extract trapped ETH via withdraw()
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every claim() call with excess ETH

operational_questions: How much ETH has accumulated in PhiFactory from excess claim payments?
Do frontends guide users to send exact amounts?
Does batchClaim have the same issue?
validation_requirements: Call claim() with msg.value = mintFee + 0.1 ether
Verify 0.1 ether remains in factory, not returned to caller
Check withdraw() sends to protocolFeeDestination not user

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack per se — happens naturally when users overpay
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Factory accumulates excess ETH over time
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Owner calls withdraw() to extract to protocolFeeDestination
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users have no recourse to recover overpayment

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: User fund loss on every overpaid claim
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery path for users; only owner can extract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: PhiFactory ETH balance growing over time
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users reporting missing ETH after claims
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Discrepancy between msg.value and mintFee in claim txs

threats.title: CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute() Sends Royalty Fee to Any Caller — Permissionless Fee Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: The distribute() function sends royaltyfee + rounding dust to msg.sender. Anyone can call distribute(credId) and receive withdrawRoyalty% (default 1%) of the total accumulated balance plus integer rounding dust. This creates a permissionless extraction mechanism where bots or opportunistic callers extract protocol fees.
threats.investigation_rationale: Royalty fees should go to the protocol or a designated recipient, not to arbitrary callers. The current design incentivizes racing to call distribute first.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _msgSender().safeTransferETH(royaltyfee + distributeAmount - actualDistributeAmount) — sends royalty to any caller

preconditions: balanceOf[credId] > 0 (rewards have accumulated)
Cred has at least one curator with shares

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Royalty fee sent to msg.sender instead of protocol destination
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time balance accumulates for a credId

operational_questions: What is the average balance accumulated per credId before distribution?
Is the 1% default royalty significant at expected volumes?
How much rounding dust leaks per distribution?
validation_requirements: Deposit 1 ETH for a credId, call distribute from arbitrary address
Verify caller receives 0.01 ETH royalty + dust
Confirm no access control check

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call distribute(credId) to extract royalty fee
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Repeat across all credIds with non-zero balances

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol loses royalty revenue to opportunistic callers

threats.title: RewardControl.withdrawFor Allows Anyone to Force-Withdraw Any User's Full Reward Balance
threats.hypothesis_description: withdrawFor(from, amount) has no access control — any external caller can trigger withdrawal of 'amount' from any address's balance, sending ETH to the 'from' address. With amount=0 (FULL_BALANCE sentinel), this forces a complete withdrawal. While funds go to the balance owner (not stolen), this disrupts withdrawal timing, can force ETH onto contracts with expensive/reverting receive(), and undermines composability with protocols expecting accumulated balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function allows unauthorized third parties to trigger financial state changes for arbitrary accounts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdrawFor(address from, uint256 amount) external — no msg.sender check; _withdraw sends to 'from' address; amount=0 triggers FULL_BALANCE withdrawal

preconditions: Target address has non-zero balance in PhiRewards
No special roles required for attacker

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No authorization check — anyone can trigger withdrawal for any account
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a user has rewards balance

operational_questions: Can forcing withdrawal onto a contract with reverting receive() DoS future deposits?
Do any composing protocols rely on accumulated PhiRewards balances?
Could this be used to grief gas-expensive receive() contracts?
validation_requirements: Call withdrawFor(victim, 0) from arbitrary address
Verify victim's full balance is withdrawn to victim's address
Test with victim being a contract with reverting receive()

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify target with accumulated rewards via balanceOf
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call withdrawFor(target, 0) to force full withdrawal
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If target is a contract without receive(), transaction reverts — balance locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If target expects to accumulate, disrupts their strategy

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Forced withdrawals disrupt timing; potential DoS for contract recipients
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low for EOAs; potentially permanent for contracts without receive()

threats.title: BondingCurve._getCreatorFee Missing Return at Supply Zero Causes Incorrect Price Quotes
threats.hypothesis_description: _getCreatorFee checks if supply_ == 0 and sets creatorFee = 0 but does NOT return. Execution falls through to the royalty calculation, overwriting creatorFee with a non-zero value. This causes getBuyPriceAfterFee and getSellPriceAfterFee to return inflated prices at supply=0, while actual trades use getPriceData which correctly returns creatorFee=0. Users and frontends relying on the AfterFee view functions see wrong prices.
threats.investigation_rationale: Price quote inconsistency between view functions and actual trade execution creates user confusion and potential economic mispricing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (supply_ == 0) { creatorFee = 0; } — no return statement; falls through to creatorFee = (price_ * royaltyRate) / RATIO_BASE

preconditions: Cred with supply=0 (creator sold their initial share, or freshly created before first buy)
User queries getBuyPriceAfterFee or getSellPriceAfterFee

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing return after setting creatorFee=0 in supply==0 branch

operational_questions: How large is the price discrepancy at supply=0?
Do frontends use getBuyPriceAfterFee for slippage calculations?
Could users be deterred from buying at supply=0 due to inflated quote?
validation_requirements: Compare getBuyPriceAfterFee(credId, 0, 1) vs actual cost from getPriceData(credId, 0, 1, true)
Verify creatorFee != 0 in _getCreatorFee despite supply=0

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe inflated price quote at supply=0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users may avoid buying or set incorrect slippage tolerance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Informed attacker buys at actual (lower) price when others are deterred

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect price quotes mislead users and frontends
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: getBuyPriceAfterFee, getSellPriceAfterFee, and any dependent UI/contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Discrepancy between quoted and actual prices at supply=0
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users overpaying or avoiding trades at supply=0

threats.title: CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute() Unbounded Loop Over All Curators Causes Permanent Fund Lock
threats.hypothesis_description: distribute() calls credContract.getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) to fetch ALL curator addresses, then loops over all of them to calculate rewards and prepare depositBatch arrays. For popular creds with hundreds+ of curators, this exceeds the block gas limit, making distribution permanently impossible and locking all accumulated curator rewards for that credId.
threats.investigation_rationale: An attacker can deliberately buy 1 share each from many addresses to inflate the curator count, or organic growth can naturally reach the gas limit threshold.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) fetches all curators; two loops over all curators; phiRewardsContract.depositBatch with unbounded arrays

preconditions: Cred has accumulated curator rewards (balanceOf[credId] > 0)
Number of unique curators exceeds gas limit threshold (~200-500 depending on gas cost)
Attacker can buy 1 share from many addresses to accelerate this

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No pagination in distribute(); fetches all curators in single call
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After sufficient curator count is reached

operational_questions: What is the maximum number of curators before distribute() exceeds gas limit?
What is the cost for an attacker to create enough curator addresses?
Is there any recovery mechanism for locked funds?
validation_requirements: Deploy with many curators, measure gas for distribute()
Determine exact gas limit threshold
Verify no alternative distribution path exists

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create many addresses and buy 1 share each for target credId
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for curator rewards to accumulate via minting activity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: distribute() now exceeds gas limit — funds permanently locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No recovery path; contract has no admin withdrawal for specific credIds
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Capital for buying shares from many addresses

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All curator rewards for affected credId permanently locked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery without contract upgrade or admin intervention
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Curator reward distribution for popular creds

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate — create and fund multiple addresses

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Many small 1-share purchases for same credId from different addresses

threats.title: Missing Reentrancy Guard on Individual buyShareCred/sellShareCred Enables Cross-Function Reentrancy
threats.hypothesis_description: _handleTrade (used by individual buy/sell) lacks the nonReentrant modifier that batch operations use. On sell, ETH is sent to the curator via safeTransferETH before protocol fees and creator fees are transferred. If the curator is a contract, its receive() can reenter Cred functions. While share balance is updated before the transfer (preventing direct re-sell), cross-function reentrancy into buyShareCred at the reduced supply could exploit the temporarily inconsistent state where protocol fees haven't been distributed yet.
threats.investigation_rationale: Batch functions have nonReentrant but individual trades do not, indicating an inconsistent security model. Multiple external calls after state updates create a window for cross-function reentrancy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _handleTrade has no nonReentrant; sell path: _updateCuratorShareBalance -> currentSupply -= amount -> curator_.safeTransferETH (REENTRANCY WINDOW) -> protocolFeeDestination.safeTransferETH -> IPhiRewards.deposit

preconditions: Seller is a smart contract with custom receive()/fallback()
Contract's receive() calls back into Cred functions
SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD must have passed for the sell

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: nonReentrant only on batch operations; individual trades unprotected
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During ETH transfer to seller in _handleTrade

operational_questions: Can a reentering buy at reduced supply create economic advantage?
Does the contract's ETH balance remain consistent during reentrancy?
Can reentrancy into createCred or other functions exploit the window?
validation_requirements: Deploy attacker contract that reenters on sell payout
Test buyShareCred reentrancy during sell execution
Verify if any state inconsistency is exploitable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy contract that calls buyShareCred in its receive()
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Buy shares, wait for lock period
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Sell shares; receive() triggers buyShareCred at reduced supply
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Buy at lower bonding curve price during reentrancy window

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After lock period on held shares

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Contract addresses selling shares with internal transactions during same tx
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unusual trade patterns in single transactions

threats.title: Artist Can Toggle Soulbound Status After Minting, Retroactively Locking or Unlocking Already-Distributed Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: PhiNFT1155.safeTransferFrom checks soulBounded(id_) which dynamically queries PhiFactory.artData(artId).soulBounded. The artist can call updateArtSettings to change soulBounded at any time, even after tokens are minted. Setting soulBounded=true retroactively makes all existing tokens non-transferable. Setting soulBounded=false on a previously soulbound token enables transfers of credential tokens that were supposed to be non-transferable.
threats.investigation_rationale: Soulbound status is a critical property that affects token economics and credential integrity. Dynamic toggling undermines user expectations and marketplace listings.

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeTransferFrom checks soulBounded(id_) which calls phiFactoryContract.artData(_tokenIdToArtId[tokenId_]).soulBounded — dynamically read from mutable storage

preconditions: Artist (art creator) decides to change soulbound status
Tokens have already been minted to holders

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Soulbound status dynamically queried from mutable factory storage, not snapshot at mint

operational_questions: Can a malicious artist list tokens as transferable, wait for secondary sales, then toggle to soulbound?
Does toggling soulbound=false on credential tokens undermine protocol integrity?
Are there marketplace implications for retroactive soulbound changes?
validation_requirements: Create art with soulBounded=false, mint tokens, toggle to true
Verify transfer reverts after toggle
Test reverse: soulBounded=true -> false -> successful transfer

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users mint and list on secondary markets
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All listed tokens become non-transferable, trapping marketplace listings

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Reversible by toggling back, but damage may be done
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All transfers and marketplace activity for affected tokens

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — single transaction by art creator

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: updateArtSettings calls changing soulBounded after numberMinted > 0
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Transfer failures for previously transferable tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Soulbound toggle correlated with marketplace activity

threats.title: BondingCurve.setCredContract Commented Out in Deployment Script Leaves credContract as address(0) Breaking All Trade Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: The deployment script (Deploy.s.sol) has bondingCurve.setCredContract(address(credProxy)) commented out. If not called post-deployment, BondingCurve.credContract remains address(0). All calls to _getProtocolFee and _getCreatorFee will revert when calling credContract.protocolFeePercent() or credContract.getCreatorRoyalty() on address(0). This means getPriceData reverts, making all Cred trade operations (buy/sell/batch) impossible.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script explicitly shows this critical configuration step was intentionally commented out. Without separate post-deployment setup, the entire trading system is non-functional.
deployment_assumptions: bondingCurve.setCredContract must be called separately after deployment

proof_of_signal.pattern: credContract is never set in constructor; getPriceData calls credContract.getCreatorRoyalty which reverts on address(0); deployment script has setCredContract commented out

preconditions: bondingCurve.setCredContract not called after deployment
Any user attempts to create a cred or buy/sell shares

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Critical configuration step commented out in deploy script with no documented alternative
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: BondingCurve owner must call setCredContract post-deployment
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From deployment until setCredContract is called

operational_questions: Is setCredContract called in a separate post-deployment step?
Is there a deployment runbook that includes this step?
Could the protocol be deployed without this step and appear functional until first trade?
validation_requirements: Check if bondingCurve.credContract is set on deployed contracts
Verify getPriceData reverts when credContract is address(0)
Confirm createCred fails because internal buyShareCred uses getPriceData

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack — deployment configuration gap
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All trade operations fail on freshly deployed system

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete trading system non-functional until configured
